source_texts: Nana, informed by Labordette of what had taken place, arrived triumphant. She affected the style of a respectable woman, with most distinguished ways, just to astonish every one and to prove to those idiots that, when she liked, not one of them could come up to her; but she almost forgot herself. Rose, as soon as she saw her, flew at her, stammering in a choking voice,

“They’re coming, here they are. And the General is making up the rear. I’ll fling open all the doors and call all my daughters, every single one, now, this minute,” Lebedev mumbled softly in a fluster, dashing from door to door.

A thousand lords with girdle-jade walk in front and rear. The many flags of guardsmen stand both left and right. Those holding gilt bludgeons, And halberds and axes, March in pairs and pairs; The red silk lanterns, The royal incense urn, Move in solemnity. The dragons fly and the phoenixes dance; The falcons soar and the eagles take wing. This Son of Heaven’s an upright sage; The righteous ministers are good. They increase our bliss by a thousand years, surpassing Yu and Shun; They secure peace of ten thousand ages, rivaling Yao and Tang. We also see the curve-handled umbrella, And robes with rolling dragons— Their glare lighting up each other; The jade joined-rings, The phoenix fans, Waving through holy mist. Those caps of pearls and belts of jade; The purple sashes and medals of gold. A thousand rows of soldiers protect the Throne; Two lines of marshals uphold the carriage. This emperor, cleansed and sincere, bows to the Buddha, Glad to raise incense and seek virtue’s fruit. The grand cortege of the Tang emperor soon arrived in front of the temple. The emperor ordered a halt to the music, left the carriages, and led many officials in the worship of Buddha by taking up burning incense sticks in their hands. After bowing three times holding the incense, they raised their heads and looked around them. This was indeed a splendid religious hall. You see Dancing flags and banners; Bright, gleaming sunshades. Dancing flags and banners Fill the air with strands of flashing colored mists. Bright, gleaming sunshades Glow in the sun as fiery bolts. Imposing, the gold image of Lokājyeṣṭha;14 Most awesome, the jade features of the arhats. Divine flowers fill the vases. Sandalwood incense burn in the urns. The divine flowers filling the vases Adorn the temple with a brilliant forest of brocade. The sandalwood incense burning in the urns Covers the clear sky with waves of fragrant clouds. Piled high on red trays are fruits in season. On colored counters, mounds of cakes and sweets rest. Rows of noble priests chant the holy sūtras To save from their travails those orphaned souls. Taizong and his officials each lifted the incense; they also worshipped the golden body of the Buddha and paid homage to the arhats. Thereafter, the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the various monks to greet the Tang emperor. After the ceremony, they went back to their seats according to their rank and station. The priest then presented Taizong with the proclamation for the deliverance of the orphaned souls. It read: The supreme virtue is vast and endless, for Buddhism is founded upon Nirvāṇa. The spirit of the pure and the clean circulates freely and flows everywhere in the Three Regions. There are a thousand changes and ten thousand transformations, all regulated by the forces of yin and yang. Boundless and vast indeed are the substance, the function, the true nature, and the permanence of such phenomena. But look at those orphaned souls, how worthy they are of our pity and commiseration! Now by the holy command of Taizong, we have selected and assembled various priests, who will engage in meditation and in the proclamation of the Law. Flinging wide the gates of salvation and setting in motion many vessels of mercy, we would deliver you, the multitudes, from the Sea of Woe and save you from perdition and from the Sixfold Path. You will be led to return to the way of truth and to enjoy the bliss of Heaven. Whether it be by motion, rest, or nonactivity, you will be united with, and become, pure essences. Therefore make use of this noble occasion, for you are invited to the pleasures of the celestial city. Take advantage of our Grand Mass so that you may find release from Hell’s confinement, ascend quickly and freely to ultimate bliss, and travel without restraint in the Region of the West.15 The poem says: An urn of immortal incense. Some scrolls of salvific power. As we proclaim this boundless Law, Receive now Heaven’s endless grace. All your guilt and crime abolished, You lost souls may leave your prison. May our nation be firmly blessed With peace long and all-embracing. Highly pleased by what he read, Taizong said to the monks, “Be firm, all of you, in your devotion, and do not slack in your service to Buddha. After the achievement of merit and after each has received his blessing, we shall reward you handsomely. Be assured that you will not have labored in vain.”
“Yes,” said Rose Mignon, “his foot caught in a trap-door and he has sprained his ankle most abominably. If you had only heard him swear, with his leg all tied up and stretched out on a chair! ”

He escorted his mother to her hotel. Afterwards, he turned onto the Moika. There was gloom in the windows of the little apartment. The Likhutins were not there. Nothing to be done: he turned and went home.

Nevertheless La Faloise would very much have liked to have known the name of the woman at whose house the supper was to take place, but the countess had recalled Vandeuvres, and was questioning him as to the way tea was made in England. He was often there, attending the races in which his horses ran. According to him, only the Russians knew how to make tea; and he mentioned their recipe. Then, as though he had been thinking very much whilst speaking, he interrupted himself to ask, “By the way, and the marquis?

She explained to him what had happened, that Fantine had been very poorly the day before, and that she was better now because she thought that monsieur le maire had gone to Montfermeil to fetch her child. The nun dared not question monsieur le maire, but from the way he looked she could see quite clearly that was not where he had come from.

Observing Conseil, I noticed that the good fellow was yielding ever so slightly to the general mood. Or at least I thought so. Perhaps, for the first time in his life, his nerves were tingling with a feeling of curiosity.

Again he produced the whole wad of bills, counted off three of them, tossed them onto the counter, and hurriedly left the store, accompanied by the shop assistants and errand boys, all of them bowing to him and wishing him a good time. Andrei cleared his throat after swallowing his brandy and jumped up onto the driver’s box. But just as Mitya was installing himself in the cart, to his great surprise, Fenya appeared. She was running, completely out of breath, and as soon as she reached him, she let out a cry and threw herself down at his feet.
“Where to?” Alyosha whispered back, glancing around him. They were alone in a smallish garden. The house was at least fifty yards away. “Why do you have to whisper?”
rationales: Outputs 1 and 4 share the same paraphrasing fingerprint: both avoid including actual quoted dialogue, instead abstractly describing the speech (e.g., 'strained, breathless tone' vs 'panicked and incoherent tone'), both use formal and generic language throughout, and both summarize events in a detached, indirect manner rather than preserving colorful or specific phrasing from the source.

Outputs 1 and 4 share the same core phrasing ('muttered softly,' 'rushing from one door to another/the other,' 'now, this minute,' and placing 'The general is bringing up the rear' as a separate closing statement). Their sentence structures, word choices, and overall stylistic patterns are nearly identical, differing only in minor details like quote style and slight word reordering.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same content, structure, and narrative arc: a grand royal procession, arrival at the temple, burning incense and bowing to Buddha, a ceremony led by Chen Xuanzang presenting a proclamation about Buddhism's virtues and compassion for orphaned souls, and the emperor rewarding the monks. Both use similar descriptive phrasing (e.g., 'curved umbrellas/curved-handled umbrellas,' 'dragon-adorned robes/robes with dragon patterns,' 'jade' accessories), and follow the same sequential organization. Output 3 is a condensed/simplified version of Output 0, consistent with two runs of the same paraphrasing system producing outputs of varying length but sharing the same stylistic fingerprint and content mapping.

Outputs 2 and 4 share nearly identical sentence structure and phrasing: 'his foot caught in a trap-door,' the conditional framing 'If you could have heard'/'If only you could hear him swearing,' and 'leg tied up and stretched/laid out on a chair.' This structural and lexical mirroring, distinct from the other outputs, points to the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 2 share nearly identical sentence structure and phrasing throughout: both open with 'After taking his mother to her hotel, he walked along the Moika,' both describe the windows as dark with 'indicating the Likhutins were [not home/out],' and both conclude with him returning home. The only differences are minor synonym substitutions ('not home' vs 'were out', 'his own residence' vs 'home'), which is a hallmark of the same paraphrasing system producing slightly varied outputs.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the same archaic/literary register and nearly identical structural choices: both open with 'Nevertheless,' both use 'would [much/still] have liked to know the name/who the woman was,' both describe an 'inner/inwardly following...train of thought,' and both use 'broke off/broke in' for the interruption. They also both omit the explicit final question/dialogue that Output 2 includes. These distinctive stylistic fingerprints — the formal register, the specific phrasing around the 'train of thought,' and the parallel sentence structures — strongly suggest they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 3 are very similar in structure and word choice. Both use nearly identical phrasing: 'Fantine had been very ill the day before and was better now because she thought that Monsieur le maire/the mayor had gone to Montfermeil to fetch/get her child' and 'The sister/she dared not question the mayor/Monsieur le maire; but she perceived plainly/saw by his looks that he had not come from there.' These two outputs share the same sentence structure, vocabulary, and flow, suggesting they came from the same paraphrasing system that stayed very close to the original source text.

Outputs 0 and 1 share very similar stylistic patterns: both use 'subtly influenced' by an 'atmosphere,' both reference Conseil's 'usual composure' wavering, and both describe curiosity as a potentially new feeling with elaborated, explanatory phrasing. Their sentence structures and word choices (subtle/subtly, composure, atmosphere, genuine curiosity) are closely parallel, distinguishing them from the other outputs which stay closer to the original text or use different phrasing strategies.

Outputs 1 and 3 share distinctive stylistic fingerprints: both use 'delivery boys' (instead of 'errand boys'), 'driver's seat' (instead of 'box'), 'cart', and both restructure the narrative in the same order (Andrei climbing up first, then Fenya appearing). Both employ a modern, clean paraphrasing style with similar sentence structures and vocabulary choices, such as 'swallowing his brandy' and the smooth, contemporary reordering of events.

Outputs 3 and 4 share the same structural pattern: they both rephrase Alyosha's question as 'Where are we headed/going?', both use the phrase 'a good distance away, at least fifty yards', and both end with a paraphrased version of the whispering question ('Why do you need to be so quiet?' vs. 'Why the hushed tone?'). They also both avoid using the word 'whisper' directly, instead opting for softer descriptors like 'cautiously/barely above a whisper' and 'softly'. These shared stylistic choices and structural similarities strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.

human_par3: At this moment Nana, to whom Labordette had brought the news, came down to the stage in triumph. She was quite the honest woman now and wore a most distinguished expression in order to overwhelm her friends and prove to the idiots that when she chose she could give them all points in the matter of smartness. But she nearly got into trouble, for at the sight of her Rose darted forward, choking with rage and stuttering:

At that very moment, Nana, who’d been informed by Labordette, came in exultantly. She was putting on her respectable lady act, full of genteel airs and graces to impress her colleagues and prove to those clots that when she wanted to, no one could be as classy as she was. But she nearly gave herself away. When Rose caught sight of her, she rushed towards her, choking and spluttering:
llama_31_8b: Nana, having been briefed by Labordette on the events that had unfolded, made a grand entrance, exuding an air of elegance and refinement that was meant to leave everyone in awe. Her intention was to show those foolish people that, on her good days, none of them could match her level of sophistication. However, she momentarily lost her composure. As soon as Rose laid eyes on her, she rushed forward, speaking in a panicked and incoherent tone.
gemini_25_flash: When Nana learned from Labordette what had occurred, she appeared victorious. She adopted the demeanor of a refined, respectable woman, aiming to impress everyone and demonstrate her superiority when she chose to exercise it; however, she nearly lost control. The moment Rose spotted her, she lunged forward, speaking in a strained, breathless tone,
dipper_lowmed: Nana, informed by Labordette of what had taken place, arrived triumphant. She affected the style of a respectable woman, with the most distinguished manners, in order to astonish everyone and prove to those idiots that when she liked, not one of them could match her; but she almost forgot herself. Rose, as soon as she saw her, rushed to her, stammering in a strangled voice, “Oh, my God!

human_par3: ‘They’re coming, sir, they’re coming. And even the general is coming, after them. I’m going open all the doors, and summon all my daughters, all of them, right now, right now,’ Lebedev whispered in fear, waving his arms and lunging from one door to the next.
llama_31_8b: Lebedev mumbled frantically as he rapidly opened all the doors, summoning every daughter he had right away.
gemini_25_flash: In a flustered state, Lebedev softly muttered, "They're arriving, here they come. The General is bringing up the rear. I'll throw open every door and summon all my daughters, each and every one, right away," as he darted from one door to another.
dipper_lowmed: ‘They are here, they are here. I’ll open all the doors and call all the daughters, every one, now, this minute,’ Lebedev muttered softly, flustered, rushing from one door to another. ‘The general is bringing up the rear.’

“They are here, they are here. I’ll throw open every door and call all my daughters, right now, this very minute,” Lebedev mumbled softly, rushing about from one door to the other. “And the general is coming up behind them.”

human_par3: There was no thunder and no lightning after this. This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.

“How wonderful!” gasped all present—including the emperor—kneeling, bowing, and burning incense. Taizong called quickly for a skilled painter to sketch a true likeness of the Bodhisattva, but as soon as the artist lifted his brush, the vision vaporized, leaving only a slip of paper drifting down on the breeze. On it was written the following message: Greetings, Emperor of the Tang! 108,000 miles west of here, wonderful texts speak of the Mahayana. Once distributed throughout your empire, these sutras will deliver the ghosts of the damned from Hell. He who is willing to seek them will become a golden Buddha. “Let’s pause the mass and our pursuit of virtue until someone has brought back these miraculous scriptures,” decided the emperor. “But who will go?” asked his officials. “Though entirely useless,” Xuanzang instantly spoke up, “I volunteer to fetch these treasures that will secure the empire in perpetuity.” The emperor immediately raised Xuanzang to his feet and bowed four times to him. “For such sacrifice and service, we will become brothers.” “I swear on my life to reach the Western Heaven,” Xuanzang responded, overwhelmed. “If not, I will never return home and instead pass straight to Hell.” The beaming emperor now returned to the palace, to prepare Xuanzang’s travel documents and await an auspicious day for his departure. Xuanzang, meanwhile, went back to his own temple, which was buzzing with the news of his pledge. “Did you really volunteer to go to the Western Heaven?” one of his disciples asked him. “The way is full of tigers, leopards, demons, and monsters, wide rivers and high mountains. Will we ever see you again?” “I have made a solemn oath,” said Xuanzang. “I must repay the emperor’s generosity by serving the country. If I fail, I will be damned to eternal perdition. Remember: The active mind conjures up demons; the stilled mind extinguishes them. I may be gone for two, three, seven years, or forever. But if the pine tree inside our gate points eastward, you’ll know that I’ll be back soon.” The next morning at court, Taizong wrote out a document describing the mission and stamped it with an imperial seal granting free passage to its bearer. “The stars are auspiciously aligned today for the start of a long journey,” the Department of Astronomy reported.
llama_31_8b:
A grand procession, led by a thousand dignitaries, made its way through the streets. They were accompanied by their guards, who held flags, halberds, and axes, and marched in pairs. The procession was accompanied by the soft glow of red silk lanterns and the fragrance of royal incense. The sound of dragons flying and phoenixes dancing filled the air, as well as the soaring cries of falcons and eagles. The emperor, known for his wisdom and righteousness, was revered by his officials. Together, they brought peace and prosperity to the land, surpassing even the legendary leaders of old. The emperor's procession was a magnificent sight, with its curved-handled umbrellas, dragon-adorned robes, and precious jade jewelry.

As the procession approached the temple, it came to a halt in front of a grand entrance. The emperor ordered his officials to prepare for a ceremony, and they took up incense sticks in their hands, bowing three times to show their respect. After the ceremony, the emperor and his officials paid homage to the Buddha's image and the arhats, who sat in serene stillness. The temple was filled with a sense of awe and reverence, as the emperor and his officials sought to cultivate virtue and gain spiritual enlightenment.

The ceremony was led by the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, who had come from a far-off land to bring the message of Buddhism to the emperor. After the ceremony, he presented the emperor with a proclamation, which declared that the true nature of reality was boundless and endless, and that the forces of yin and yang governed the universe. The proclamation also spoke of the importance of compassion and mercy, and how the emperor's officials had come to seek salvation and enlightenment.

The emperor, pleased with the proclamation, praised the monks for their devotion and hard work. He promised to reward them generously for their efforts, and encouraged them to continue their service to the Buddha. The emperor's words were a testament to his commitment to Buddhism and his desire to bring peace and prosperity to his people.
gemini_25_flash:
A grand procession, featuring a multitude of high-ranking officials adorned with expensive ornaments, advanced with flags and various weapons like bludgeons, halberds, and axes. Red silk lanterns and an ornate incense urn were carried with great solemnity. Symbolic creatures like dragons, phoenixes, falcons, and eagles were depicted, representing the righteousness of the Emperor and his virtuous ministers. These leaders were celebrated for bringing prosperity and lasting peace, surpassing legendary rulers of the past. The procession also included luxurious items such as curved umbrellas and robes with dragon patterns, all shining brightly. Ornate jade rings and phoenix-feather fans, moving amidst a sacred mist, further added to the grandeur. The officials wore caps of pearls and jade belts, with purple sashes and gold medals. A vast number of soldiers and marshals accompanied the Emperor’s carriage, demonstrating his profound respect as he prepared to worship Buddha.

Upon arrival at the temple, the Emperor commanded a halt to the music, disembarked, and, accompanied by his officials, reverently offered burning incense to the Buddha. After bowing three times, they observed the magnificent temple. Colorful flags and banners fluttered, creating a shimmering, multi-hued haze, while bright sunshades glowed like fiery flashes. Within the impressive hall stood a commanding golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and awe-inspiring jade figures of arhats. Vases overflowed with divine flowers, forming a vibrant tapestry, and sandalwood incense burned in urns, releasing fragrant clouds that enveloped the clear sky. Offerings of seasonal fruits on red trays and piles of cakes and sweets on decorative counters were also present. Rows of esteemed priests chanted sacred scriptures, dedicated to bringing peace to departed souls.

Following their act of worship, Emperor Taizong and his officials were greeted by Chen Xuanzang, the Master of the Law, and other monks. After the greeting, they returned to their designated places. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the spiritual liberation of orphaned souls. The proclamation emphasized Buddhism's profound and endless virtue, rooted in Nirvana, and how its pure essence pervades all realms, influencing countless transformations governed by yin and yang. It expressed deep compassion for orphaned souls and announced that, by Emperor Taizong’s decree, priests had been gathered to meditate and spread the Dharma. Their purpose was to open paths to salvation and offer compassion, freeing the multitudes from suffering and the cycle of rebirth, guiding them towards truth and heavenly bliss. It urged the souls to embrace this opportunity to attain a pure state, enjoy celestial pleasures, escape the torments of hell, and ascend to ultimate bliss in the Western Paradise.

A accompanying poem spoke of the transformative power of incense and scriptures, emphasizing the boundless grace of Heaven, the abolition of all guilt, and the release of lost souls from their metaphorical prison, culminating in blessings of lasting peace for the nation. Deeply impressed, Taizong encouraged the monks to remain steadfast in their devotion and service to Buddha, promising generous rewards for their efforts and achievements. He assured them that their hard work would not go unrewarded.
dipper_lowmed: It is a joy that surpasses mountains and rivers; An era of peace that lasts ten thousand years, That rivals the great Yu and the venerable Shun. You see A thousand lords with girdles of jade Marching before and behind; A thousand pairs of halberdiers and axes; A thousand pairs of spears and swords; A thousand pairs of ephouses and hats of gold.

human_par3: "Yes," said Rose Mignon, "his foot caught in a trap door, and he's got a fearful sprain. If only you could hear him swearing, with his leg tied up and laid out on a chair!"
‘Yes’, said Rose, ‘he got his foot caught in a trapdoor and gave himself a nasty sprain… You should have heard him swearing, with his leg bandaged up and stretched out on a chair.’
llama_31_8b: Rose Mignon confirmed that her brother had fallen into a trapdoor, severely spraining his ankle. He had been in great pain, and his language was particularly colourful while he was on the chair with his leg stretched out.
gemini_25_flash: "Indeed," Rose Mignon replied, "he tripped on a trap-door and severely twisted his ankle. You should have heard the curses he uttered while his leg was bandaged and propped up on a chair!"
dipper_lowmed: “Yes,” said Rose Mignon, “his foot caught in a trap-door, and he has sprained his ankle most abominably. If you could have heard him swearing with his leg tied up and stretched out on a chair!”

human_par3: Nikolai Apollonovich took his mother to her hotel; then—he turned off to the Moika; in the windows of the apartment was darkness: the Likhutins were not at home; nothing for it: he turned back home.
llama_31_8b: He walked his mother to her hotel and then headed down the Moika. When he returned to the Likhutin apartment, he noticed the curtains were drawn and the place was dimly lit. It seemed the Likhutins were out, so he ended up going back home instead.
gemini_25_flash: After taking his mother to her hotel, he walked along the Moika. The small apartment's windows were dark, indicating the Likhutins were not home. With no other options, he went back to his own residence.
After taking his mother to her hotel, he walked along the Moika. The small apartment's windows were dark, indicating the Likhutins were out. Since there was nothing he could do, he went back home.
dipper_lowmed: He escorted his mother to her hotel, then turned into the Moika. There was nothing to do: he turned back and went home. There was darkness in the windows of the little apartment; the Likhutins were not there.

The sister dared not question the mayor; but she perceived plainly from his air that he had not come from there.
llama_31_8b: The nun recounted the previous day's events to him, revealing that Fantine's condition had deteriorated significantly. However, the nun had noticed an improvement in Fantine's health that morning, attributing it to her assumption that the mayor had traveled to Montfermeil to retrieve her child. Despite her reserved nature, the nun was suspicious of the mayor's true intentions, sensing that his presence was not a result of a trip to the child's location, based on his demeanor.

Fantine had been unwell the previous day, but was recovering now because she had been told that the mayor had gone to retrieve her child from Montfermeil. However, the nun sensed that the mayor's story didn't add up, based on the suspicious look on his face.

human_par3: In looking at Conseil, I could see he was undergoing a little of the general influence. At least I thought so. Perhaps for the first time his nerves vibrated to a sentiment of curiosity.
llama_31_8b: I watched Conseil closely and noticed a subtle change in his demeanor, suggesting he was beginning to be influenced by the collective atmosphere. It was possible that his usual composure was giving way to a spark of genuine curiosity, a feeling he might never have experienced before.

As I watched Conseil, I sensed that he was being subtly influenced by the group's atmosphere, his usual composure wavering ever so slightly. I couldn't be certain, but it seemed that he might be experiencing a novel sense of curiosity, a feeling that was possibly unnerving him.
gemini_25_flash: I observed Conseil and it seemed to me that he was, however subtly, being influenced by the prevailing atmosphere. It struck me that, possibly for the very first time, he was experiencing a genuine sense of curiosity.

responses: At that very moment, Nana, who’d been informed by Labordette, came in exultantly. She was putting on her respectable lady act, full of genteel airs and graces to impress her colleagues and prove to those clots that when she wanted to, no one could be as classy as she was. But she nearly gave herself away. When Rose caught sight of her, she rushed towards her, choking and spluttering:

In a flustered state, Lebedev softly muttered, "They're arriving, here they come. The General is bringing up the rear. I'll throw open every door and summon all my daughters, each and every one, right away," as he darted from one door to another.

A grand procession, featuring a multitude of high-ranking officials adorned with expensive ornaments, advanced with flags and various weapons like bludgeons, halberds, and axes. Red silk lanterns and an ornate incense urn were carried with great solemnity. Symbolic creatures like dragons, phoenixes, falcons, and eagles were depicted, representing the righteousness of the Emperor and his virtuous ministers. These leaders were celebrated for bringing prosperity and lasting peace, surpassing legendary rulers of the past. The procession also included luxurious items such as curved umbrellas and robes with dragon patterns, all shining brightly. Ornate jade rings and phoenix-feather fans, moving amidst a sacred mist, further added to the grandeur. The officials wore caps of pearls and jade belts, with purple sashes and gold medals. A vast number of soldiers and marshals accompanied the Emperor’s carriage, demonstrating his profound respect as he prepared to worship Buddha.

Upon arrival at the temple, the Emperor commanded a halt to the music, disembarked, and, accompanied by his officials, reverently offered burning incense to the Buddha. After bowing three times, they observed the magnificent temple. Colorful flags and banners fluttered, creating a shimmering, multi-hued haze, while bright sunshades glowed like fiery flashes. Within the impressive hall stood a commanding golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and awe-inspiring jade figures of arhats. Vases overflowed with divine flowers, forming a vibrant tapestry, and sandalwood incense burned in urns, releasing fragrant clouds that enveloped the clear sky. Offerings of seasonal fruits on red trays and piles of cakes and sweets on decorative counters were also present. Rows of esteemed priests chanted sacred scriptures, dedicated to bringing peace to departed souls.

Following their act of worship, Emperor Taizong and his officials were greeted by Chen Xuanzang, the Master of the Law, and other monks. After the greeting, they returned to their designated places. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the spiritual liberation of orphaned souls. The proclamation emphasized Buddhism's profound and endless virtue, rooted in Nirvana, and how its pure essence pervades all realms, influencing countless transformations governed by yin and yang. It expressed deep compassion for orphaned souls and announced that, by Emperor Taizong’s decree, priests had been gathered to meditate and spread the Dharma. Their purpose was to open paths to salvation and offer compassion, freeing the multitudes from suffering and the cycle of rebirth, guiding them towards truth and heavenly bliss. It urged the souls to embrace this opportunity to attain a pure state, enjoy celestial pleasures, escape the torments of hell, and ascend to ultimate bliss in the Western Paradise.

A accompanying poem spoke of the transformative power of incense and scriptures, emphasizing the boundless grace of Heaven, the abolition of all guilt, and the release of lost souls from their metaphorical prison, culminating in blessings of lasting peace for the nation. Deeply impressed, Taizong encouraged the monks to remain steadfast in their devotion and service to Buddha, promising generous rewards for their efforts and achievements. He assured them that their hard work would not go unrewarded.

Rose Mignon confirmed that her brother had fallen into a trapdoor, severely spraining his ankle. He had been in great pain, and his language was particularly colourful while he was on the chair with his leg stretched out.
Nikolai Apollonovich took his mother to her hotel; then—he turned off to the Moika; in the windows of the apartment was darkness: the Likhutins were not at home; nothing for it: he turned back home.

Nevertheless, la Faloise would still have liked to know who the woman actually was, but the countess had again called Vandeuvres away to tell her about the English method of tea-making. He made frequent visits to England, where he raced his horses. In his opinion the Russians were the only people who could make tea properly, and he explained their method. Then, as if he’d been following an inner train of thought while he was talking, he broke off and asked:

The nun recounted the previous day's events to him, revealing that Fantine's condition had deteriorated significantly. However, the nun had noticed an improvement in Fantine's health that morning, attributing it to her assumption that the mayor had traveled to Montfermeil to retrieve her child. Despite her reserved nature, the nun was suspicious of the mayor's true intentions, sensing that his presence was not a result of a trip to the child's location, based on his demeanor.

As I watched Conseil, I sensed that he was being subtly influenced by the group's atmosphere, his usual composure wavering ever so slightly. I couldn't be certain, but it seemed that he might be experiencing a novel sense of curiosity, a feeling that was possibly unnerving him.

But as Mitya was getting into the cab, to his great surprise, Fenya appeared, completely out of breath, and as soon as she reached him, cried out and threw herself at his feet. Again he produced the whole wad of bills, threw three of them on the counter and hurried out of the shop, followed by the shop assistants and errand boys, all bowing to him and wishing him good luck.

‘But where?’ Alyosha asked, also in a whisper, gazing around him to all sides and observing that he found himself in a completely empty garden, in which apart from the two of them there was no one. The garden was small, but there were at least some fifty yards between them and the mistress’s little house. ‘ Anyway, there’s no one here, so why are you whispering?’

When Nana learned from Labordette what had occurred, she appeared victorious. She adopted the demeanor of a refined, respectable woman, aiming to impress everyone and demonstrate her superiority when she chose to exercise it; however, she nearly lost control. The moment Rose spotted her, she lunged forward, speaking in a strained, breathless tone,

“They are here, they are here. I’ll throw open every door and call all my daughters, right now, this very minute,” Lebedev mumbled softly, rushing about from one door to the other. “And the general is coming up behind them.”

“How wonderful!” gasped all present—including the emperor—kneeling, bowing, and burning incense. Taizong called quickly for a skilled painter to sketch a true likeness of the Bodhisattva, but as soon as the artist lifted his brush, the vision vaporized, leaving only a slip of paper drifting down on the breeze. On it was written the following message: Greetings, Emperor of the Tang! 108,000 miles west of here, wonderful texts speak of the Mahayana. Once distributed throughout your empire, these sutras will deliver the ghosts of the damned from Hell. He who is willing to seek them will become a golden Buddha. “Let’s pause the mass and our pursuit of virtue until someone has brought back these miraculous scriptures,” decided the emperor. “But who will go?” asked his officials. “Though entirely useless,” Xuanzang instantly spoke up, “I volunteer to fetch these treasures that will secure the empire in perpetuity.” The emperor immediately raised Xuanzang to his feet and bowed four times to him. “For such sacrifice and service, we will become brothers.” “I swear on my life to reach the Western Heaven,” Xuanzang responded, overwhelmed. “If not, I will never return home and instead pass straight to Hell.” The beaming emperor now returned to the palace, to prepare Xuanzang’s travel documents and await an auspicious day for his departure. Xuanzang, meanwhile, went back to his own temple, which was buzzing with the news of his pledge. “Did you really volunteer to go to the Western Heaven?” one of his disciples asked him. “The way is full of tigers, leopards, demons, and monsters, wide rivers and high mountains. Will we ever see you again?” “I have made a solemn oath,” said Xuanzang. “I must repay the emperor’s generosity by serving the country. If I fail, I will be damned to eternal perdition. Remember: The active mind conjures up demons; the stilled mind extinguishes them. I may be gone for two, three, seven years, or forever. But if the pine tree inside our gate points eastward, you’ll know that I’ll be back soon.” The next morning at court, Taizong wrote out a document describing the mission and stamped it with an imperial seal granting free passage to its bearer. “The stars are auspiciously aligned today for the start of a long journey,” the Department of Astronomy reported.

‘They’re coming, sir, they’re coming. And even the general is coming, after them. I’m going open all the doors, and summon all my daughters, all of them, right now, right now,’ Lebedev whispered in fear, waving his arms and lunging from one door to the next.

A grand procession, led by a thousand dignitaries, made its way through the streets. They were accompanied by their guards, who held flags, halberds, and axes, and marched in pairs. The procession was accompanied by the soft glow of red silk lanterns and the fragrance of royal incense. The sound of dragons flying and phoenixes dancing filled the air, as well as the soaring cries of falcons and eagles. The emperor, known for his wisdom and righteousness, was revered by his officials. Together, they brought peace and prosperity to the land, surpassing even the legendary leaders of old. The emperor's procession was a magnificent sight, with its curved-handled umbrellas, dragon-adorned robes, and precious jade jewelry.

As the procession approached the temple, it came to a halt in front of a grand entrance. The emperor ordered his officials to prepare for a ceremony, and they took up incense sticks in their hands, bowing three times to show their respect. After the ceremony, the emperor and his officials paid homage to the Buddha's image and the arhats, who sat in serene stillness. The temple was filled with a sense of awe and reverence, as the emperor and his officials sought to cultivate virtue and gain spiritual enlightenment.

The ceremony was led by the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, who had come from a far-off land to bring the message of Buddhism to the emperor. After the ceremony, he presented the emperor with a proclamation, which declared that the true nature of reality was boundless and endless, and that the forces of yin and yang governed the universe. The proclamation also spoke of the importance of compassion and mercy, and how the emperor's officials had come to seek salvation and enlightenment.

The emperor, pleased with the proclamation, praised the monks for their devotion and hard work. He promised to reward them generously for their efforts, and encouraged them to continue their service to the Buddha. The emperor's words were a testament to his commitment to Buddhism and his desire to bring peace and prosperity to his people.

This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.
